title: Frustrated magnetic order in hybrid Kitaev spin-orbital models
authors: Ivan Dutta, Aayush Vijayvargia, Anamitra Mukherjee, Onur Erten, Kush Saha
abstract: Spin-orbital generalization of Kitaev model provides a robust extension to the original Kitaev model. However, real materials often exhibit competing interactions that break exact solvability which can give rise to new phases. Motivated by recent microscopic proposals of coexisting Yao-Lee and Kitaev couplings, we investigate the fate of the ground state when two independent exactly solvable spin liquid Hamiltonians each originally formulated on different lattice geometries are combined on a common lattice environment. We first focus on the hybrid Kitaev's honeycomb and square-lattice model. Using self-consistent mean-field analysis and perturbative calculation, we show that the strong-Kitaev regime yields magnetic order in the spin sector, while the orbital sector retains its topological order. We further analyze the hybridization of the Yao-Lee and square-lattice models and find that the model exhibits a rich evolution of Majorana Dirac bands and Lifshitz transitions. Remarkably, when the Yao-Lee and square-lattice couplings are equal and opposite, the model restores its exact solvability with a single itinerant Majorana flavor. These results demonstrate that hybrid spin liquid platforms may host various emergent phases beyond conventional exactly solvable limits.
summary: This paper explores what happens when two different types of quantum spin liquids are merged into a single lattice. It reveals that the system can undergo 'magnetic fragmentation,' where magnetism and topological order coexist, and shows how tuning interactions can lead to complex phase transitions and restored exact solvability.
main_problem: Investigating the emergent ground states and phase transitions that occur when two independent, exactly solvable spin-orbital Hamiltonians (Kitaev and Yao-Lee) are combined on a common lattice geometry.
main_result: The study discovers 'magnetic fragmentation,' where the spin sector develops magnetic order while the orbital sector retains topological order, and identifies a special point where the model restores exact solvability.
method: Self-consistent Majorana mean-field analysis, perturbative calculations in the strong-Kitaev limit, and Luttinger-Tisza analysis for determining ordering wavevectors.
model_or_system: A hybrid spin-orbital model on a modified honeycomb/square lattice featuring both Kitaev-type interactions and square-lattice-type interactions involving spin and orbital degrees of freedom.
key_observables: Magnetization (m_lambda), bond order parameter (chi_lambda), Majorana Dirac bands, Lifshitz transitions, and plaquette flux sectors.
important_parameters: Kitaev coupling strength (J_K), square-lattice coupling strength (J_S), and the ratio of Yao-Lee to square-lattice couplings (J_Y/J_S).
main_assumptions: Use of mean-field decoupling for quartic terms and a unit cell approximation to handle incommensurate magnetic structures.
figures_summary: Schematic of the modified honeycomb lattice, color plots of Majorana conduction bands showing Lifshitz transitions, and energy per site plots for the mean-field solutions.
paper_structure: Introduction to spin-orbital models, description of the hybrid Kitaev and Yao-Lee Hamiltonians, mean-field methodology, analysis of the Kitaev-square regime (magnetic fragmentation), analysis of the Yao-Lee-square regime (band evolution), and discussion of the exact solvability point.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Each lattice site (with sublat- tice A and B) hosts independent spin σ and orbital τ degrees of freedom. The operators Wp and Ws,s′ denote the counterclockwise defined conserved hexagonal and square pla- quettes, respectively. (b) A geometrically equivalent square- lattice representation, illustrating that all the aforementioned definition remains same. Figure 2. Mean field order parameters across JK/JS for JK < 0. Results are derived from the self-consistent mean-field solution of Eq. 12. Across all panels, the bond order parameters χ are represented by solid lines, whereas the magnetic order parameters m and N are visualized via the background color maps. (a), (b) shows the bond order parameter of 1,2,3 bond χα for JK/JS < 0 and > 0 respectively. Similarly (c), (d) shows the bond order parameter for the fourth bond χ4. For JK/JS < 0 in the left column, the system transitions into an antiferromagnetic phase, with the intensity of the blue background indicating the Neel order N y. Figure 3. (a) Colourplot of the lowest energy eigenspectrum of HLT (k) to find kmin. The two red crosses indicate the exact coordinates of the optimal ordering wavevectors, kmin = (0, ±1.207). (b) Schematic of the unit cell employed for the self-consistent mean-field analysis in the JK > 0. The dashed line represents the exact incommensurate spatial periodicity, 2π/kmin, determined by LT method. This is approximated by the commensurate 4-site unit cell highlighted in grey. The plus and minus signs on the lattice sites denote the relative signs of the local magnetizations, my A and my B, for the spiral antiferromagnetic regime.

Figure 4. Mean field order parameters across JK/JS for JK > 0. Results are derived from the self-consistent mean-field solution of Eq. 12. Across all panels, the bond order parameters χ are represented by solid lines, whereas the magnetic order parameters m and N are visualized via the background color maps. (a), (b) shows the bond order parameter of 1,2 bond χ1,2 for JK/JS < 0 and > 0 respectively. Similarly (c), (d) shows the bond order parameter for the third bond χ3 and (e), (f) for the fourth bond χ4. For JK/JS < 0 in the left column, the system transitions into a spiral ferromagnetic phase, with the intensity of the red background indicating the magnitude of the magnetization |mx|....

Figure 5. Mean field order parameters as a function of JY /JS obtained by solving Eq. 18 self-consistently at JS = −1. Fig- ure (a) and (b) depict the behavior of the fourth-bond order parameter χ4 alongside the corresponding bond orders for the 1, 2, and 3 bonds χα, respectively.
